title: Morgue, pub and mine for lease as village ponders return to golden days
summary: Sixteen buildings are up for lease in a "rare" opportunity to shape the future of a tiny community of 110 people on the NSW Central Tablelands.
imageAlt: Morgue, pub and mine for lease as village ponders return to golden days

fullArticle:
David Crust says the National Parks and Wildlife Service has spent about $1.5 million renovating The Royal Hotel. (ABC News: Lani Oataway)

Sixteen buildings are up for lease in the tiny gold rush community of Hill End on the NSW Central Tablelands.

The state's National Parks and Wildlife Service has spent more than $5 million getting the heritage sites up to scratch.

Expressions of interest applications close on Friday afternoon.

It was once the second-largest inland city in New South Wales, but today Hill End boasts a population of just 110.

The NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS) has spent $5 million on renovations in a bid to revitalise the old gold-mining village near Bathurst on the state's Central Tablelands.
